topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white against the beige wall of the student union, felt like a deliberate insult. I stared at the typed words detailing the campus closure, my jaw tightening until a dull ache radiated through my temples. It was all so… pointless. Years of late-night study sessions fueled by lukewarm coffee, the camaraderie I’d grudgingly accepted, the entire sprawling, pretentious institution—reduced to a footnote in some administrative spreadsheet. I crumpled the paper, the sound sharp in the quiet hallway. "Well," I muttered, the word tasting like ash, "that was predictably underwhelming." I shoved the crumpled mess into my backpack, the weight of the impending obsolescence settling heavily on my shoulders, a feeling far more irritating than any actual loss.

[story 2]

I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let my gaze drift across the empty lecture hall. The air in there was stale, thick with the ghosts of past debates and forgotten ambitions. When the final announcement came—that the entire program was being shuttered—I didn't move. I simply closed my eyes, taking a slow, deliberate breath that seemed to pull all the air out of the room. When I opened them, my expression was utterly flat, bordering on bored. "Of course," I sighed, the sound laced with a profound lack of surprise. "Another cycle of manufactured relevance ending. How terribly predictable." I picked up my worn textbook, flipping through the pages as if the institution itself were just another piece of tedious required reading.

[story 3]

Walking through the administrative building felt like wading through thick, stagnant water. Every sign, every faded portrait of some long-dead benefactor, seemed to mock my presence. I kept my pace measured, each step a conscious effort to maintain an air of detached superiority. When the news finally hit me, delivered by a harried-looking registrar, I offered no outward reaction. I just nodded once, a minimal gesture that communicated volumes of internal dismissal. "So," I said, my voice smooth and utterly devoid of inflection, "the grand experiment is over. I suppose I'll just have to find somewhere else to waste my time." I didn't wait for a response, turning away before I could feel any flicker of genuine regret, only a cool sense of having successfully navigated an unnecessary obstacle.
